trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 1 training completed in ~9.4s (2 epochs), but evaluation failed because PyTorch 2.6 defaults torch.load to weights_only=True, which rejects the numpy arrays stored in the checkpoint (mean, std, adj_norm). Analysis: This is a loading compatibility issue, not a model quality issue. The saved checkpoint is fine. Decision: Update predict.py to use weights_only=False, verify the checkpoint loads, and immediately re-submit the existing checkpoint for evaluation to establish a baseline score.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline achieved mae_60min=4.4535 with a tiny 2-layer, hidden=64 model trained for only 2 epochs. Each epoch takes ~3-4s, so training is extremely fast. Analysis: The model is severely underfitting. There is ample headroom to increase capacity, add regularization, and train longer. A strong inductive bias for time-series is to residualize predictions around the last observed value. Additionally, weighting the L1 loss linearly from 1x to 2x across the 12 output steps will directly pressure the network to improve the 60-minute horizon (step 12), which is the primary metric. Decision: Upgrade model to hidden=256, layers=3, dropout=0.2, add a last-timestep residual connection, switch to AdamW with weight decay, use weighted L1 loss, batch_size=128, and train for 20 epochs. Then submit evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The larger model (hidden=256, layers=3, dropout=0.2) trained for 20 epochs achieved mae_60min=4.2428, improving over the 4.4535 baseline. Training loss still declining (0.59) and validation standard-L1 is flat (~0.37), indicating no overfitting and room for more capacity. Analysis: The network is underfitting; we can increase width/depth, add an adaptive adjacency matrix, use gated temporal convolutions (GLU), aggregate multi-layer skip connections, and more aggressively weight the later time steps in the loss (1x→3x). Training throughput is very high (~18s/epoch), so a 40-epoch run with a 512×4 model (~9M params) will take ~12-15 minutes—well within budget. Decision: Rewrite model.py with adaptive graph, GLU, skip connections; update train.py with hidden=512, layers=4, dropout=0.3, weight decay=5e-4, input noise augmentation, stronger horizon weighting, and 40 epochs. Then submit eval.

Decision: Submit the current best checkpoint for evaluation immediately without additional training to measure mae_60min and decide on subsequent scaling.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 60 --batch_size 256 --lr 0.003 --weight_decay 1e-3 --hidden 512 --layers 4 --dropout 0.35
trajectory.error_summary: rch but unallocated. If reserved but unallocated memory is large try setting PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True to avoid fragmentation.  See documentation for Memory Management  (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/cuda.html#optimizing-memory-usage-with-pytorch-cuda-alloc-conf)
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 6 evaluation achieved mae_60min=3.9361 (new best), down from 4.2428. Training curves show validation loss plateaued around 0.366-0.372 after epoch 29, while training loss (weighted) still declined, suggesting the model is fitting but not yet overfitting. Analysis: There is headroom to improve step-12 accuracy by (1) adding temporal self-attention after the spatial-temporal blocks to capture long-range timestep dependencies, (2) replacing the single output conv with a small MLP, (3) aggressively reweighting the loss so step 12 gets 5× weight vs 1× for early steps, (4) increasing batch size to 256 and using mixed precision for faster throughput, and (5) adopting cosine annealing with 5-epoch warmup and a higher base LR (3e-3). These changes directly target the 60-min horizon while keeping total training time around 30-40 min. Decision: Update model.py and train.py with the above improvements, train for 60 epochs, and immediately submit the best checkpoint for evaluation in the same iteration.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7 training failed with CUDA OOM using batch_size=256 on the 512-hidden, 4-layer model that now includes temporal attention. Analysis: The attention block reshapes to [B×N, T, C], which roughly doubles activation memory. Halving batch_size to 128 should bring peak memory comfortably under the 31 GB limit while preserving the architectural upgrades (temporal attention, stronger horizon weighting, cosine LR). Decision: Rewrite train.py with batch_size=128, fix the truncated syntax, keep all other hyperparameters, suppress per-epoch loss prints (write to metrics.jsonl only), and relaunch training for 60 epochs. Immediately submit the resulting checkpoint for evaluation.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 50 --batch_size 256 --lr 0.003 --weight_decay 1e-3 --hidden 512 --layers 4 --dropout 0.2
trajectory.error_summary: rch but unallocated. If reserved but unallocated memory is large try setting PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True to avoid fragmentation.  See documentation for Memory Management  (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/cuda.html#optimizing-memory-usage-with-pytorch-cuda-alloc-conf)
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 8 produced mae_60min=4.8603, significantly worse than the best 3.9361. Training curves are completely flat (train_loss ~0.9, val_loss ~0.4 for all epochs), indicating the model failed to learn. Analysis: The main change from the working checkpoint (iter 6) was the addition of TemporalAttention and increased dropout to 0.35. The attention likely caused optimization instability or gradient issues, and the aggressive dropout may have been too much. The best checkpoint was also overwritten. Decision: Revert model.py to the proven architecture (adaptive graph + GLU + skip connections, NO temporal attention). Use batch_size=256 (should fit without attention and speed up training), dropout=0.2, and train for 50 epochs with the same cosine schedule and horizon-weighted loss. This should restore learning and hopefully improve upon 3.9361 within the remaining ~52 minutes.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 35 --batch_size 64
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 crashed with CUDA OOM using batch_size=256 on the 512h/4l model. The best score remains mae_60min=3.9361 from iteration 6, but the checkpoint was likely overwritten by later failed runs. We have ~52 minutes left. Analysis: The proven architecture (adaptive graph + GLU + skip connections, no attention) works, but needs a smaller batch size to fit memory. Iteration 5 trained this size successfully in ~50 min for 40 epochs, likely with batch_size=64. We must retrain to recover a strong checkpoint. Decision: Rewrite train.py with batch_size=64, 35 epochs (should finish in ~45 min), cosine LR with warmup, and the same proven model.py. Also rewrite predict.py to ensure compatibility. Train and submit eval immediately after.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 12 evaluation failed with CUDA OOM inside predict.py at the Conv2d input projection. The full test set of 6,832 samples was passed in one forward pass to the 512-hidden, 4-layer model, creating intermediate activations of ~34 GB—exceeding the 31 GB VRAM limit. Analysis: This is purely an inference batching bug; the training script already used batch_size=64. The checkpoint from iteration 10 (best val_loss 0.3600) is still on disk and should be strong. No retraining is needed or feasible in the remaining ~22 minutes. Decision: Fix predict.py to process test_x in batches of 64 with autocast and periodic empty_cache to avoid fragmentation. Run a quick local dummy inference to verify load/forward compatibility, then immediately submit the existing checkpoint for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best mae_60min is 3.7138 from iteration 12. We have ~21 minutes and 17 iterations left. Analysis: Instead of risky long training from scratch, the highest-ROI strategy is: (1) compute a per-step/per-sensor additive bias correction on the validation set using the existing best model, (2) fine-tune a second model starting from the best checkpoint for ~15 epochs with a lower LR and more aggressive late-horizon loss weights, (3) update predict.py to ensemble both models, apply bias correction, and use test-time augmentation (TTA). Analysis: In iteration 14, a per-step/per-sensor additive bias was computed on the validation set specifically for best.pt, but it was hidden (renamed to .bak) during iteration 15 to measure the raw baseline.
